reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Child wellbeing depends on predictable, stable school pathways and access to quality instruction appropriate to the child's pace. Parents of young children prioritise: (1) transparency and fairness in selection so families understand the stakes early; (2) reduction of test-prep pressure on 10-year-olds; (3) assurance that admission reform does not create hidden gatekeeping (informal selection, reputation-based sorting) that families cannot navigate. These values derive from documented stakeholder discourse on selection-age anxiety and from child-development evidence on age-appropriate assessment.
reactions.interest: Parents of children aged 6–12 in Hungary, particularly those with limited prior experience navigating selective admission or test-prep markets.
reactions.public_good_frame: A fair admission process that opens selective places to high-ability children regardless of family SES, reduces standardised-test pressure on young children, and maintains transparent, rule-based gatekeeping rather than informal selection.
reactions.argument: S1 appeals conditionally because it promises to reduce test-prep arms-race pressure on 10-year-olds and to widen access to selective places for able children whose families cannot afford coaching. However, the scenario itself documents that every key lever rests on unverified assumptions: a workable SES indicator does not yet exist in Hungary; a less-coachable assessment has not been designed or validated; and documented outreach may not raise application rates among low-SES families. For parents, this creates a risk that reform will *relabel* rather than *reduce* sorting, and that informal gatekeeping (interviews, reputation) will emerge in place of transparent exam rules. Parents of young children also depend on predictability: a reform that promises to reduce pressure but fails to deliver, or that shifts gatekeeping into opaque channels, leaves families uncertain about how to prepare their children and undermines trust. The conditional support holds only if: (a) the assessment redesign is piloted with genuine coaching-sensitivity testing and results are published before rollout; (b) the SES proxy is audited for gaming-resistance and alternatives are tested; (c) outreach is documented with application-rate tracking so families can see whether the promised access actually materialises.
reactions.condition_to_change: Full support contingent on: (1) published psychometric pilot data showing the new assessment is materially less sensitive to coaching than the current exam; (2) proof that any SES proxy survives auditing for gaming and is not simply replaced by informal gatekeeping; (3) documented evidence within 2 years that outreach raises application rates among low-SES and Roma families by a measurable margin (>5 percentage point increase). Shift to opposition if: (a) informal selection (interviews, school reputation vetting) visibly increases in selective schools; (b) the reform rolls out without pilots or published evidence; (c) outreach is announced but not tracked or evaluated.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parents of young children value predictability, stability, and fairness in school assignment. The phased phase-down offers the appeal of gradual change that respects existing families' expectations (grandfathering). However, it creates acute uncertainty for families whose children will experience the transition: will my child's cohort still have access to a selective place? Will the remaining selective seats become even more SES-skewed as numbers shrink? Will non-state schools absorb the demand, creating a two-tier system that requires private funds to access? For working parents, commuting and school location matter: if general-school upper grades are not distributed equitably across settlements, the phase-down may force families to travel farther or to private alternatives.
reactions.interest: Parents of children aged 6–12, particularly those in rural and peri-urban areas and those without established networks in selective schools.
reactions.public_good_frame: A gradual transition that keeps a larger share of each cohort together in mixed-ability upper grades, reducing the risk of rural/disadvantaged-school decline, while honouring existing families' expectations and not creating hidden pressure to seek non-state alternatives.
reactions.argument: S2 is conditionally supported because gradual phase-down respects family expectations (important for younger siblings' planning) and the mechanical retention of a larger cohort in the common track reduces the risk that selective expansion concentrates all achievement and resources in a shrinking network. However, the scenario documents that non-state maintainers will almost certainly absorb displaced selective demand—this is the 'dominant risk'—which would recreate SES sorting at private cost. For parents of young children, this creates a perverse outcome: the reform shrinks state selective places but does not reduce inequality, it simply relocates it to non-state schools that require family funds. The scenario also does not address whether capacity in general-school upper grades will be distributed fairly across urban and rural areas; if retained pupils cluster in urban general schools while rural schools shrink further, families in small towns face longer commutes or pressure to move—a hidden cost of the phase-down. The condition of support is that non-state offset is actively monitored and either prevented by regulation or compensated by ensuring that general-school quality and capacity are secured in all settlements.
reactions.condition_to_change: Conditional support holds only if: (1) the cap on state-maintained selective seats is paired with a binding intake limit on non-state maintainers, with published annual audit of church/foundation selective admission; (2) capacity expansion in general-school upper grades is planned and budgeted per municipality, with published settlement-level capacity targets; (3) teacher recruitment incentives are in place for general schools in rural and disadvantaged areas to ensure quality does not degrade during the transition. Shift to opposition if: (a) non-state maintainers visibly expand selective intake to absorb displaced demand and the Ministry does not intervene; (b) general-school capacity is concentrated in urban areas, leaving rural families with longer commutes or non-state alternatives; (c) the phase-down proceeds without published targets for rural/disadvantaged-school teacher staffing.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parents of young children benefit most from a system in which selection is delayed, because it reduces the stakes and anxiety of the age-10-12 transition. A comprehensive lower-secondary structure that delays formal selection to age 14-15 aligns with child-development evidence that academic trajectories are not yet stable at age 10 and that early sorting can amplify minor differences into lasting inequality. However, parents also depend on political durability and teacher quality: if the reform is reversed (Poland-style) after 5 years, families face disruptive re-sorting. If teacher supply is insufficient, comprehensive upper grades may default to large, impersonal classes that disadvantage younger learners who need differentiated support.
reactions.interest: Parents of children aged 6–12, particularly those with younger siblings who would enter under the new system and those in rural/disadvantaged areas where early selection often triggers cohort drain.
reactions.public_good_frame: A comprehensive lower-secondary structure that keeps cohorts together longer, reduces selection-related anxiety and family stratification at age 10-12, allows children more time to develop, and supports rural schools by reducing early cohort loss—conditional on political durability, teacher-quality investment, and early support systems that prevent hidden sorting via school choice or non-state exit.
reactions.argument: S3 is conditionally supported because delaying formal selection reduces the immediate stakes and anxiety of the age-10-12 transition—a documented concern for parents of young children. Keeping a cohort together in a comprehensive structure to age 14-15 also protects rural primary schools from early cohort drain and may create more mixed-ability peer groups in upper grades, benefiting younger learners who need exposure to higher-achieving peers. The scenario's evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006) shows early tracking harms equity; delayed selection is the mechanism. However, the scenario documents three critical risks that could undermine benefit: (1) middle-class and church/foundation exit to non-state schools may simply relocate sorting rather than remove it, leaving comprehensive schools as residual cohorts; (2) political reversal within 8 years (Poland precedent) would disrupt families' expectations and force re-sorting mid-schooling; (3) teacher shortages are a 'strong binding constraint'—if comprehensive upper grades are understaffed or staffed with less-qualified teachers, the reform may harm rather than help younger learners in those grades. For parents of young children, the reform's success depends entirely on whether these three risks are actively managed: non-state exit is limited by regulation or acceptance as inevitable but acceptable; political ownership is locked in via cross-party agreement or constitutional protection; and teacher supply is stabilised before (not after) rollout, with explicit salary and working-condition improvements for comprehensive-school teachers.
reactions.condition_to_change: Conditional support holds only if, *before* national rollout: (1) a single-settlement or multi-district pilot runs for at least 5 years with published outcome evaluation (peer effects, achievement, SES-outcome gradient) compared to a matched control; (2) non-state maintainer intake is explicitly regulated (intake capped or required to maintain SES diversity) during the pilot, with published admission audit; (3) a cross-party or constitutional commitment to the comprehensive structure is secured, with terms for reversal explicit if published; (4) teacher retraining and salary commitments are costed and secured before the first cohort enters comprehensive upper grades, with explicit monitoring of exit rates. Shift to opposition if: (a) the reform is announced without a pilot or with a pilot <5 years; (b) non-state maintainer intake is not regulated and visibly increases during the transition; (c) political ownership is not secured across parties and reversal becomes plausible within a decade; (d) teacher supply is not stabilised and comprehensive upper grades open with unfilled posts or with cohorts taught by unretrained teachers.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Parents of young children are most concerned with reducing selection pressure and anxiety at age 10-12 and with fairness: the rules of school admission should be transparent and stable, and selection should not systematically exclude able children whose families lack resources. S4 leaves the age-10 selection intact, which means the reform does not address the documented driver of parent anxiety and family test-prep arms-race. The scenario's own evidence acknowledges that 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age'—meaning compensation cannot undo the harm of early sorting. For working parents of young children, the policy also creates a two-tier risk: if compensation funding is not durable (the scenario flags 'budget-vulnerability' as a 'serious durability risk'), the reform amounts to a temporary subsidy to disadvantaged schools that will be cut in the next fiscal crisis, leaving families doubly harmed (both sorted at age 10 *and* abandoned when funding ends).
reactions.interest: Parents of children aged 6–12, particularly those in disadvantaged areas and those without private resources for tutoring or non-state alternatives.
reactions.public_good_frame: A fair selection process that does not penalise families for lack of resources and does not require children to be stratified at age 10-12 into selective and general tracks. Compensation for existing sorting cannot replace structural reform; it extends the harm (selection pressure) while promising future relief (improved general-school outcomes) that is neither guaranteed nor durable.
reactions.argument: S4 is opposed because it leaves the root problem—selection at age 10—unchanged. For parents of young children, this means the reform does *not* reduce the documented test-prep pressure and selection anxiety that motivates many families to seek alternatives. The scenario's own evidence states that 'no country has demonstrated offsetting at Hungary's selection age', which means compensation cannot undo the harm of early sorting; it can only reduce its downstream effects—too late for the child who experienced the sorting stress at age 10. Moreover, the scenario flags 'ring-fenced equity funding is the first cut in fiscal consolidation' as a plausible risk; for parents, this creates a moral hazard: the reform promises that disadvantaged schools will improve, but there is no guarantee the funding will persist. If it is cut in year 3 or 5, families are left with selection at age 10 *and* deteriorating general-school support—worse than before. The scenario also notes that 'compensation may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine', meaning the reform risks legitimising early selection as acceptable if disadvantaged schools are subsidised, reducing pressure to make structural change. For parents of young children, this is a critical failure: the reform abandons the prospect of delaying selection (which reduces anxiety) and instead doubles down on a system that harms younger learners.
reactions.condition_to_change: Shift to conditional support only if: (1) the TEIP-style funding is constitutionally protected or embedded in multi-party agreement, with explicit lock-in against budget cuts; (2) the scenario is paired with a credible roadmap to *eventually* delay or abolish age-10 selection (i.e., S4 becomes an interim measure with an explicit exit to S3); (3) outcome evaluation in disadvantaged schools demonstrates within 5 years that compensatory investment materially reduces the SES-achievement gap (not merely raises absolute outcomes), *and* that this gain persists even if selection remains at age 10. Without these conditions, S4 is seen as a politically convenient delay that abandons young children to continued sorting pressure while hoping future subsidies will repair the damage.
